detail: US-Israeli airstrikes hit the Mahshahr Special Petrochemical Zone in southwestern Iran, wounding at least five people and expanding attacks on Iran's energy and industrial infrastructure.
dl: HEAVY — Major petrochemical hub hit

title: Drone strike hits Iraq's North Rumaila oilfield
detail: Two drones struck the BP-operated North Rumaila oilfield in southern Iraq, wounding three Iraqi workers and underscoring the spillover of Iran-linked energy attacks into Iraq.
dl: MODERATE — Iraq oilfield hit by drones

title: Kuwait Shuwaikh oil complex and state sites hit by drones
detail: Iranian drones struck Kuwait's Shuwaikh oil sector complex, sparking a fire at the Kuwait Petroleum Corporation headquarters area, while additional drones hit power and government sites in the capital.
dl: HEAVY — Kuwaiti oil, power and government sites targeted

satelliteImages.description: Direct satellite image shows oil off the coast of Kuwait after Iranian attacks on fuel and petrochemical facilities

title: Pakistan circulates immediate ceasefire plan to end hostilities
detail: Pakistan exchanged a two-stage proposal with Iran and the United States that would trigger an immediate ceasefire, reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and then move into 15-20 days of talks on a broader settlement tentatively dubbed the 'Islamabad Accord'. Reuters reported all elements needed agreement on April 6, with no immediate commitment yet from Tehran.
dl: DIPLOMATIC — Immediate ceasefire framework circulated

title: Iran rejects temporary ceasefire, demands permanent end to war
detail: Tehran rejected the Pakistan-brokered immediate ceasefire proposal and instead sent a 10-clause response demanding a permanent end to the conflict, sanctions relief, a Strait of Hormuz safe-passage protocol, and reconstruction commitments.
dl: DIPLOMATIC — Iran rejects temporary truce and raises broader end-war terms

title: Sharif University AI data centre damaged in Tehran strike
detail: Iran said a U.S.-Israeli attack damaged an artificial intelligence data centre and other facilities at Sharif University of Technology in Tehran, one of the country's top science institutions, prompting vows of retaliation.
dl: MODERATE — University research facilities hit in Tehran

title: Israel reports wave of strikes on Iranian government infrastructure
detail: The Israeli military said it completed a wave of overnight airstrikes targeting Iranian government infrastructure in Tehran and other areas across Iran as both sides escalated ahead of Trump's deadline.
dl: HEAVY — New wave of strikes on Iranian state infrastructure

title: Iran missile threat triggers simultaneous alerts across Gulf states
detail: The UAE, Saudi Arabia and Bahrain issued public safety alerts as Iranian missile threats prompted air-defence activity, sirens and shelter warnings across the Gulf.
dl: STRATEGIC — Regional missile threat triggers UAE, Saudi and Bahrain alerts

title: Israeli strikes sever rail bridges across Iran before deadline
detail: In the hours before Trump's 8 p.m. ET deadline, Israel said it struck eight bridges across Iran and warned civilians off rail travel. Iranian state media said a railway bridge strike in Kashan killed at least three people, as the late pre-deadline bombardment widened infrastructure disruption.
dl: HEAVY — Rail bridge network struck ahead of ceasefire push

title: US, Iran and Israel agree to a two-week ceasefire
detail: Washington, Tehran and Israel accepted a Pakistan-mediated two-week ceasefire tied to reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Iran said it would pause its 'defensive operation' for the period and negotiations were expected to begin in Islamabad, though Israel said the pause did not extend to fighting with Hezbollah in Lebanon.
dl: DIPLOMATIC — Two-week ceasefire announced with Hormuz reopening

title: Habshan gas complex suspends after interception debris sparks fires
detail: After the ceasefire announcement, the UAE said falling debris from an intercepted Iranian attack caused multiple fires at Abu Dhabi's Habshan gas complex. Operations were suspended and three people, two Emiratis and one Indian national, were reported with minor injuries.
dl: MODERATE — Gas operations suspended after attack debris ignites fires

title: Israel launches heaviest Lebanon strikes since Iran ceasefire
detail: Hours after the two-week Iran ceasefire took effect, Israel carried out its largest coordinated strike of the Lebanon war, hitting more than 100 Hezbollah sites in Beirut, the Bekaa Valley and southern Lebanon. Reuters reported Lebanon's civil defence service said 254 people were killed and more than 1,100 wounded, while Hezbollah responded early the next day with rockets toward northern Israel.
tn: Beirut and wider Lebanon strike wave
dl: CRITICAL — Deadliest day in Lebanon war tests Iran ceasefire

title: Hormuz stays restricted as Iran issues mine-avoidance routes
detail: The Strait of Hormuz remained shut to vessels sailing without an Iranian permit even after the ceasefire announcement. Reuters reported the Revolutionary Guards navy published alternative shipping routes to help vessels avoid naval mines, reinforcing that Gulf energy flows were still constrained while diplomacy remained shaky.
dl: STRATEGIC — Strait remains restricted with mine-risk routing guidance

title: Hezbollah missile on Haifa prompts fresh Israeli strikes in Lebanon
detail: Reuters reported Hezbollah launched a missile toward Haifa that was intercepted, while Israel said it struck 10 launchers in Lebanon after rocket fire toward northern Israel. The exchange underscored the still-unresolved dispute over whether the Iran ceasefire also covers Lebanon.
dl: LOW — Cross-border fire continues despite wider ceasefire

title: Iran says war death toll has exceeded 3,000
detail: A senior Iranian forensic official said more than 3,000 people had been killed in enemy attacks during the war, marking Tehran's clearest public casualty estimate yet. AP reported the figure as negotiators prepared for U.S.-Iran talks in Islamabad under an increasingly fragile ceasefire.
dl: MILESTONE — Iran publicly pegs war deaths above 3,000

title: Islamabad peace talks open under threat of collapse
detail: Senior U.S. and Iranian delegations reached Islamabad for the highest-level talks between the two countries in decades, but Tehran said negotiations could not properly begin without U.S. commitments on sanctions relief and a Lebanon ceasefire. The standoff made the wider Iran war ceasefire look increasingly fragile even as direct U.S.-Iran diplomacy finally started.
dl: STRATEGIC — Direct U.S.-Iran talks begin but are immediately strained

title: Hezbollah operations and Israeli overflights keep Lebanon front active
detail: Reuters reported Israeli strikes resumed in southern Lebanon on Saturday morning, with drones and warplanes operating over Beirut, while Hezbollah announced several attacks on Israeli positions in Lebanon and northern Israel. The exchanges showed the Lebanon theater was still active despite the broader U.S.-Iran ceasefire and ahead of planned Israel-Lebanon talks in Washington.
dl: LOW — Active cross-border operations continue on the Lebanon front

title: Islamabad ceasefire talks end without a deal
detail: Marathon U.S.-Iran negotiations in Islamabad ended after roughly 21 hours without an agreement, leaving the two-week ceasefire in doubt. Reuters and AP both reported the main sticking points included Iran's nuclear program and control of the Strait of Hormuz, with Pakistan urging both sides to preserve the truce.
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S.-Iran peace talks collapse and ceasefire stability worsens

title: Israeli strike kills six in Maaroub, southern Lebanon
detail: AP reported that an Israeli strike hit a home in the town of Maaroub near Tyre on Sunday morning, killing six people. Reuters separately reported Israeli strikes on Hezbollah rocket launchers overnight and renewed rocket alerts in Israeli border communities, underscoring that the Lebanon front remained active despite the wider ceasefire.
dl: MODERATE — Deadly Israeli strike hits Maaroub as Lebanon fighting continues

title: Saudi restores East-West pipeline to full capacity
detail: Saudi Arabia said it restored the East-West oil pipeline to about 7 million barrels per day and recovered energy facilities damaged in attacks during the Iran war. Reuters said the route had become the kingdom's only crude export corridor after the Strait of Hormuz closed, making the recovery a major regional energy development.
dl: STRATEGIC — Saudi export route recovers after wartime attacks

title: U.S. announces blockade of Iranian ports after talks fail
detail: After the Islamabad negotiations collapsed, President Trump said the U.S. Navy would begin blockading maritime traffic entering and leaving Iranian ports starting Monday, while still allowing transit to non-Iranian ports through the Strait of Hormuz. AP and Reuters both said the move sharply escalated pressure on Tehran and prompted Iranian officials to warn that military vessels in the strait would face a forceful response.
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S. orders a naval blockade of Iranian ports after failed ceasefire talks

title: U.S. says blockade of Iranian ports is fully in effect
detail: U.S. Central Command said no ships got past the new blockade in its first 24 hours and that six merchant vessels were ordered to turn back toward Iranian waters. Reuters and AP both reported the move marked the first actual enforcement phase of the blockade Trump announced after the Islamabad talks collapsed, with more than a dozen warships and thousands of U.S. personnel involved.
tn: Strait of Hormuz / Gulf of Oman
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S. blockade shifts from announcement to active enforcement at Iran's ports

title: U.S. hosts first direct Israel-Lebanon talks in decades
detail: Secretary of State Marco Rubio hosted Israeli and Lebanese envoys in Washington in the first direct talks between the two countries in decades. Reuters and AP said the meeting produced agreement to continue direct negotiations, even though Lebanon pressed for a ceasefire while Israel kept demanding Hezbollah's disarmament and did not commit to ending its attacks.
dl: DIPLOMATIC — Rare direct Israel-Lebanon talks open a new channel around the wider war

title: U.S. threatens secondary sanctions as blockade chokes Iran sea trade
detail: Reuters reported the first 48 hours of the U.S. blockade stopped all vessels from passing in or out of Iranian ports, with nine ships ordered back toward Iranian waters. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Washington could impose secondary sanctions on buyers of Iranian oil and had warned two Chinese banks not to process Iranian money, sharply escalating the economic pressure campaign tied to the war ceasefire talks.
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S. blockade tightens into a broader financial squeeze on Iran

title: Israel weighs Lebanon ceasefire as Hezbollah fires 40 rockets
detail: Reuters reported Israel's security cabinet met to discuss a possible Lebanon ceasefire under U.S. pressure, even as fighting continued. Hezbollah launched about 40 rockets into Israel on Wednesday morning, while Netanyahu said Israeli forces were closing in on Bint Jbeil and the army signaled it wanted all territory south of the Litani River to become a no-go zone for Hezbollah operatives.
dl: LOW — Ceasefire talks advance while cross-border fighting continues

title: Iran floats Omani-side Hormuz transit proposal in peace talks
detail: Reuters reported Tehran proposed allowing ships to sail through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack if a deal is reached with Washington. The offer marked Iran's first visible step back from earlier threats to impose tolls or unilateral control over the waterway, while hundreds of ships and roughly 20,000 seafarers remained stranded by the war disruption.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran signals limited flexibility on Hormuz access in exchange for a deal

title: Pakistani mediator returns to Tehran as ceasefire hopes revive
detail: Reuters reported Pakistan's army chief Asim Munir arrived in Tehran to narrow gaps between Iran and the United States after the Islamabad talks failed, while Washington said prospects for a deal looked good and Trump said more talks between the two sides would happen on Thursday. The renewed mediation raised hopes for a broader end to the war and a possible Lebanon ceasefire, even though no final agreement had yet been announced.
dl: DIPLOMATIC — Pakistan re-enters the talks as expectations of a deal rise again

title: Israel-Lebanon 10-day ceasefire takes effect
detail: A U.S.-brokered 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon took effect at 2100 GMT on April 16 to open negotiations toward a permanent security agreement. Reuters reported celebratory gunfire in Beirut as the truce began, while Lebanese authorities accused Israel of intermittent shelling in the opening hours, underscoring how fragile the pause remained.
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S.-brokered truce opens a narrow path to wider regional de-escalation

title: 40-country talks launched on postwar Hormuz security mission
detail: France and Britain chaired talks among about 40 countries on a strictly defensive multinational mission to restore freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz once conditions allow. Reuters reported the meeting would address stranded seafarers, trapped commercial vessels and planning for escorts, mine-clearing and intelligence sharing after the war or a lasting ceasefire.
dl: STRATEGIC — Allies begin planning a multinational mission to reopen Hormuz

title: US and Iran near memorandum of understanding
detail: Reuters reported Pakistani mediation produced progress on the main sticking points and that a coming U.S.-Iran meeting could yield a memorandum of understanding, followed by a broader deal within 60 days. The report said compromise was emerging on Iran's enriched uranium stockpile and the duration of a nuclear pause, raising the strongest sign yet that the war could move toward a negotiated end.
dl: STRATEGIC — Backchannel talks edge toward a draft deal to end the war

title: First tanker convoy crosses Hormuz since war began
detail: Reuters reported a convoy of eight tankers, including a VLCC, product tankers, chemical tankers and LPG carriers, was crossing the Strait of Hormuz on April 18 under a limited managed-passage arrangement coordinated by Iran. It was the first major outbound tanker movement since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began in late February, signaling a partial reopening of the waterway even as many ships remained stuck and transit was still tightly restricted.
dl: STRATEGIC — First major tanker convoy moves through Hormuz after seven weeks of wartime disruption

title: Iran reimposes Hormuz restrictions and IRGC fires on ships
detail: Hours after the first managed tanker convoy crossed the Strait of Hormuz, Iran restored strict controls over the waterway and warned that no ships could pass while the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports remained in force. Reuters and AP reported that Iranian gunboats opened fire on at least two merchant vessels, including Indian-linked shipping, and radioed other ships that the strait had effectively been shut again.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran reverses the partial reopening and uses force to reassert control over Hormuz traffic

title: U.S. seizes Iranian cargo ship near Hormuz
detail: The United States said a Navy destroyer intercepted and disabled the Iranian-flagged cargo ship Touska near the Gulf of Oman after it tried to run the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports. Reuters and AP described it as the first such seizure since the blockade began, while Iran denounced the boarding as piracy and threatened retaliation, putting the fragile ceasefire and planned Pakistan talks at risk.
tn: Gulf of Oman / Strait of Hormuz approaches
dl: STRATEGIC — First U.S. blockade seizure sharply raises the risk of ceasefire collapse

title: Israel publishes first map of new south Lebanon control line
detail: Reuters reported that the Israeli military for the first time published a map showing its new deployment line inside southern Lebanon, placing dozens of mostly abandoned villages under Israeli control only days after the Lebanon ceasefire took effect. The move underscored that the truce remained fragile and that the Lebanon front was still reshaping the wider Iran war's endgame.
dl: STRATEGIC — Israel formalizes a new line of control inside southern Lebanon after the ceasefire

title: Israel expands south Lebanon exclusion warnings to border belt and Litani line
detail: Reuters reported that Israel warned residents not to return to a belt of territory running the full southern Lebanon border and not to approach the Litani River, entrenching its hold even after the 10-day ceasefire began. The order signaled that the Lebanon front remained volatile and that Israel was turning its newly published control line into an active exclusion zone.
dl: STRATEGIC — Israel hardens post-ceasefire control with a broader exclusion warning across southern Lebanon

title: Pakistan says Iran gave a positive signal on new US talks
detail: A senior Pakistani official told Reuters that Islamabad had received a positive signal from Tehran and was confident it could bring Iran into fresh talks with the United States. The disclosure marked the clearest indication yet that the post-seizure Hormuz crisis had not collapsed the mediation track, even though Iranian officials were still publicly hedging.
dl: STRATEGIC — Pakistan keeps the ceasefire diplomacy alive after the Hormuz ship seizure shock

title: Trump extends Iran ceasefire while keeping port blockade in place
detail: Reuters reported that President Donald Trump indefinitely extended the U.S.-Iran ceasefire on April 21 at Pakistan's request to keep peace talks alive, but said the U.S. Navy would continue blockading Iranian ports and coastlines. Tehran did not clearly accept the move and Iranian hardliners warned the blockade itself remained an act of war, making the truce extension a major diplomatic development rather than a clean de-escalation.
dl: STRATEGIC — Ceasefire is prolonged, but the continuing U.S. blockade keeps a major flashpoint alive

title: Hezbollah fires on Israeli buffer-zone forces, prompting Israeli strike
detail: Live war coverage on April 21 reported that Hezbollah fired rockets and drones at Israeli forces maintaining the new buffer zone in southern Lebanon, and Israel responded by striking the launcher and calling the attack a ceasefire violation. The exchange showed that the Lebanon front remained active even after the 10-day ceasefire took effect and despite parallel efforts to stabilize the wider Iran war.
dl: LIGHT — Post-ceasefire rocket and drone fire triggers an immediate Israeli retaliation

title: Iran seizes two cargo ships in the Strait of Hormuz
detail: IRGC forces fired on three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, seized the Epaminondas and MSC Francesca, and escorted the two ships toward Iranian shores. The move marked Iran's first wartime ship seizures since the conflict began and tightened its de facto closure of the waterway even as the broader U.S.-Iran ceasefire remained murky.
dl: HEAVY — Iran escalates the maritime war by openly seizing commercial shipping

title: U.S. intercepts Iranian oil tankers in Asian waters
detail: Reuters reported that U.S. forces intercepted at least three Iranian-flagged oil tankers near India, Malaysia and Sri Lanka and redirected them away from their positions as part of Washington's maritime blockade. The interdictions showed the blockade expanding well beyond Hormuz itself into the wider Indian Ocean energy trade.
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S. blockade enforcement expands into Asian waters

title: Israeli strikes make April 22 the deadliest Lebanon ceasefire day yet
detail: Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon killed at least four people in Reuters' initial reporting and at least five by the broader Iran-war update, while Hezbollah launched a drone at Israeli forces and accused Israel of violating the ceasefire. The clashes made April 22 the deadliest day since the 10-day Lebanon ceasefire began on April 16 and underscored how fragile the parallel Lebanon track remained.
dl: MODERATE — Deadliest Lebanon ceasefire day so far, with strikes and a Hezbollah drone attack

title: Iran refuses to reopen Hormuz while U.S. blockade continues
detail: Reuters reported that Tehran said it would not consider reopening the Strait of Hormuz or resuming meaningful peace talks until Washington lifts its blockade of Iranian shipping. The demand hardened the central ceasefire standoff after Iran's April 22 ship seizures and left Pakistan-brokered diplomacy stalled despite Trump's indefinite ceasefire extension.
dl: STRATEGIC — Iran ties reopening Hormuz and renewed talks to removal of the U.S. maritime blockade

title: Trump orders Navy to destroy Iranian mine-laying boats in Hormuz
detail: President Trump said he had ordered the U.S. Navy to "shoot and kill" Iranian boats laying mines in the Strait of Hormuz and to step up demining operations, while claiming Washington was in "total control" of the sealed waterway. The order raised the risk of a direct maritime clash even as the formal U.S.-Iran ceasefire remained in place.
dl: HEAVY — U.S. naval rules of engagement escalate around Iranian mine-laying activity

title: U.S. boards tanker Majestic as blockade total reaches 33 redirected vessels
detail: Reuters reported that U.S. forces boarded another Iran-associated tanker, the Majestic, in the Indian Ocean near Sri Lanka and that the U.S. military said it had redirected 33 vessels since the blockade began. The action showed the blockade expanding into a sustained interdiction campaign against Iranian-linked shipping far beyond the Strait of Hormuz.
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S. blockade enforcement reaches 33 redirected vessels and another boarded tanker

title: Israel and Lebanon extend Hezbollah ceasefire by three weeks
detail: After White House talks with Israeli and Lebanese representatives, Trump announced that Israel and Lebanon had agreed to extend the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire by three weeks. The extension kept the Lebanon front formally paused after several deadly violations, though Reuters noted it remained unclear how much control Beirut could exert over Hezbollah.
dl: STRATEGIC — Three-week extension keeps the Lebanon front contained despite recent clashes

title: Third U.S. carrier enters the regional theater
detail: U.S. Central Command said the USS George H.W. Bush had arrived in the Indian Ocean inside its area of responsibility, bringing the number of American aircraft carriers operating around the Iran war theater to three. The arrival reinforced Washington's blockade and strike posture while Trump said the clock was ticking for Iran to accept a wider deal.
tn: Indian Ocean / CENTCOM carrier operating area
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S. naval presence grows to three aircraft carriers in and around the war theater

title: Araqchi reaches Islamabad as U.S. sends Witkoff and Kushner for Iran talks
detail: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi arrived in Islamabad to consult Pakistan on proposals for restarting peace talks, while the White House said Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner would depart for Pakistan on Saturday. Trump told Reuters that Iran planned an offer aimed at satisfying U.S. demands, but Tehran said it did not plan to meet U.S. representatives directly, leaving Pakistan as the immediate intermediary.
dl: STRATEGIC — Pakistan mediation reopens a possible talks channel, but direct U.S.-Iran contact remains disputed

satelliteImages.description: Vantor high-resolution satellite imagery shows a C-130-type aircraft at Pakistan's Nur Khan airbase, assessed as Iran-linked during the Islamabad peace-talks period
Vantor imagery shows two additional aircraft resembling Iranian Meraj Air planes at Nur Khan airbase during Iranian delegation visits to Islamabad

title: Only five ships transit Hormuz in 24 hours as blockade standoff holds
detail: Reuters shipping data showed only five ships, including one Iranian oil products tanker, passed through the Strait of Hormuz in the previous 24 hours, compared with roughly 130-140 daily passages before the war. The trickle of traffic, with hundreds of ships and roughly 20,000 seafarers stranded inside the Gulf, showed that the strait remained effectively unusable for normal energy trade despite the ceasefire.
dl: HEAVY — Hormuz traffic remains a tiny fraction of normal flows, deepening the global energy disruption

title: Lebanon ceasefire frays as Israeli strikes kill two and Hezbollah downs drone
detail: A day after Israel and Lebanon extended their ceasefire, Lebanon reported two people killed by an Israeli strike in Touline, Hezbollah said it shot down an Israeli Hermes 450 drone, and Israel warned residents of Deir Aames to leave before a planned operation against alleged Hezbollah activity. Hezbollah called the ceasefire meaningless while Israeli forces continued to hold a 5-10 km buffer zone in southern Lebanon.
dl: MODERATE — Fighting continues immediately after the ceasefire extension, including deaths and a downed drone

title: Drones launched from Iraq hit Kuwaiti border posts
detail: Kuwait's army said two drones launched from Iraq struck two northern border posts, causing material damage but no casualties. Iraq condemned the attack and ordered an investigative committee, while the incident signaled a potential spillover risk from Iran-aligned militia networks along the Gulf's northern edge.
dl: LIGHT — Drone strikes cause damage on Kuwait’s border and raise regional spillover concerns

title: U.S. sanctions Chinese teapot refinery and shadow-fleet vessels over Iranian oil
detail: The U.S. Treasury sanctioned Hengli Petrochemical (Dalian) Refinery, calling it one of Iran's largest crude and petroleum-products customers, and also targeted about 40 shipping companies and vessels tied to Iran's shadow fleet. The measure broadened Washington's economic campaign around the maritime blockade as peace talks were expected to resume through Pakistan.
dl: STRATEGIC — U.S. pressure expands to a major Chinese buyer and Iran-linked shadow-fleet shipping

title: Iran hangs alleged Mossad agent over Isfahan protest sabotage
detail: Iran said it executed Erfan Kiani, whom Tasnim described as a Mossad-linked operative, after accusing him of vandalism, arson and violence during nationwide protests earlier in the year in Isfahan. The execution underscored Tehran's internal-security crackdown and its use of alleged Israeli intelligence ties as the war and ceasefire diplomacy continued.
dl: STRATEGIC — Execution of an alleged Israeli agent signals intensified domestic counterintelligence pressure during the war

title: U.S. destroyer stops Iranian-flagged ship in Hormuz blockade enforcement
detail: U.S. Central Command released a photo of the USS Rafael Peralta tracking and stopping an Iranian-flagged vessel in the Strait of Hormuz as it tried to sail to an Iranian port. U.S. War Secretary Pete Hegseth said American forces had forced 34 ships to turn around without incident since the blockade began, keeping pressure on Iranian maritime access even as talks moved to Pakistan.
dl: MODERATE — Direct U.S. naval interdiction continues to enforce the blockade and restrict Iranian port access

title: Islamabad locks down as Iran and U.S. teams prepare indirect talks
detail: Pakistan placed parts of Islamabad under heavy security as Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi met Pakistani military and interior officials and U.S. envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner headed to the city. Reports said the first contacts would run through separate Pakistani-mediated meetings, with a possible direct U.S.-Iran encounter on Sunday if the opening round succeeded, while Tehran publicly denied that direct talks were planned.
dl: STRATEGIC — Pakistan mediation becomes the main diplomatic channel, though the sides still dispute whether direct talks will occur

satelliteImages.description: High-resolution Vantor imagery reportedly shows an Iranian Air Force C-130 parked at Nur Khan Airbase near Islamabad during Pakistan-mediated Iran-U.S. talks

title: Israel strikes Hezbollah rocket launchers north of Lebanon security zone
detail: The Israeli Air Force said it struck Hezbollah rocket launchers overnight in Deir ez-Zahrani, Kfar Reman and Sammaaiyeh, north of Israel's forward defense line in southern Lebanon, saying the launchers posed a threat to troops and civilians. The strikes came alongside renewed IDF warnings for Lebanese civilians not to return south toward the Litani-area villages despite the extended ceasefire.
dl: MODERATE — Israeli airstrikes continue inside Lebanon despite the ceasefire extension, keeping the northern front unstable

title: Tehran international airport resumes commercial flights for first time since war began
detail: Iranian state television reported that commercial flights resumed from Imam Khomeini International Airport in Tehran for the first time since the U.S.-Israel conflict began about two months earlier, with departures to Istanbul, Muscat and Medina. The limited reopening followed Iran's partial airspace reopening earlier in April and suggested a cautious move toward civilian normalization under the ceasefire.
dl: STRATEGIC — Limited flight restoration marks a tentative normalization step after nearly two months of wartime disruption

title: Iran offers Hormuz reopening plan while deferring nuclear talks
detail: Iranian negotiators circulated a new proposal through mediators that would end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz while postponing negotiations over Iran's nuclear program to a later phase. U.S. officials said President Trump reviewed the offer with national-security aides but remained focused on freedom of navigation and preventing Iran from retaining highly enriched uranium, leaving the ceasefire diplomacy stalled.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran tests a ceasefire formula that would relieve maritime pressure without front-loading nuclear concessions

title: Hezbollah FPV drone kills Israeli soldier near Taybeh
detail: Hezbollah used first-person-view drones against Israeli forces around Taybeh in southern Lebanon, killing one IDF soldier and wounding six others. ISW assessed that Hezbollah had increasingly shifted to FPV and fiber-optic drones since the Lebanon ceasefire, using them as its main precision strike platform against Israeli ground units.
dl: SEVERE — Fiber-optic FPV attacks are producing Israeli casualties and undermining the Lebanon ceasefire line

title: Iran seeks Russian backing as Araghchi meets Putin in St. Petersburg
detail: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi met Russian President Vladimir Putin in St. Petersburg as Tehran prepared for either renewed fighting or further negotiations. Putin said Moscow would do what it could to help secure peace, while ISW noted that Russia had supported Iran during the war, including by facilitating Iranian targeting through intelligence and satellite support.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran turns to Moscow for diplomatic and security backing as ceasefire talks wobble

title: Iran denounces U.S. seizure of two Iranian-linked oil tankers
detail: Iran's Foreign Ministry condemned the U.S. seizure of two tankers loaded with Iranian oil as piracy and armed robbery, after U.S. officials publicized the action as part of maritime law-enforcement and blockade pressure. The dispute sharpened the naval dimension of the war as the United States continued blocking Iranian exports while Iran kept restrictions around Hormuz.
dl: SEVERE — U.S. seizure of Iranian-linked cargo escalates the blockade from interdiction into direct economic capture

title: Marines board M/V Blue Star III during blockade enforcement
detail: U.S. Central Command said Marines from the 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit boarded the commercial vessel M/V Blue Star III in the Arabian Sea on suspicion it was trying to reach Iran in violation of the U.S. blockade. The ship was released after inspection confirmed it would not make an Iranian port call, while CENTCOM said 39 vessels had been redirected to ensure compliance.
dl: MODERATE — U.S. boarding operations show the blockade expanding into active inspection of commercial shipping

title: U.S. sanctions Iran shadow-banking network supporting armed forces
detail: The U.S. Treasury imposed sanctions on 35 people and entities accused of operating Iran's shadow-banking architecture, saying the network moved billions of dollars tied to oil sales, weapons procurement and funding for Iranian proxies. Treasury warned foreign financial institutions that facilitating the networks could bring severe consequences.
dl: SEVERE — Washington broadens wartime pressure from the naval blockade into Iran's covert finance channels

title: Israel detonates Hezbollah tunnel network in Qantara
detail: The Israeli military destroyed what it said was a Hezbollah tunnel network in Qantara, producing a blast large enough for Israel's Geological Survey to register it as a seismic event. Israel said the complex contained two large tunnels with living facilities and launchers aimed at Israel, and Prime Minister Netanyahu said operations against Hezbollah infrastructure were not finished.
dl: HEAVY — Large-scale demolition of Hezbollah underground infrastructure deepens the destructive Lebanon campaign despite ceasefire terms

title: U.S. seeks to loan 92.5 million barrels from strategic reserve
detail: The Trump administration solicited exchanges for up to 92.5 million barrels of crude oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, saying the loan program was meant to calm oil markets that had spiked because of the Iran war and Hormuz disruption. The emergency market intervention underscored how the conflict was feeding through global fuel prices even while the military ceasefire held.
dl: STRATEGIC — Washington is tapping strategic oil reserves at major scale to blunt the energy shock from the Iran war and Hormuz closure

title: Tehran air defenses activate against small aircraft and drones
detail: Tehran's air defenses were activated late Thursday to counter small aircraft and drones, according to live reporting carried by The Hindu. The alert came as the ceasefire remained formally in place but U.S.-Iran talks stayed deadlocked, underscoring the risk of renewed strikes or covert drone activity around the capital.
dl: LOW — Capital air-defense activation shows persistent military readiness despite the nominal ceasefire

title: White House says Iran ceasefire ended hostilities for War Powers clock
detail: A senior Trump administration official said the early-April U.S.-Iran ceasefire had 'terminated' hostilities for purposes of the War Powers Resolution's 60-day deadline, even as the U.S. blockade and Iranian restrictions on Hormuz continued. Republicans deferred to the White House and Congress left town without moving to authorize or halt the conflict.
dl: STRATEGIC — Washington reframes the war legally while maintaining coercive blockade operations outside formal authorization

title: IRGC warns renewed U.S. attacks would bring 'long and painful strikes'
detail: A senior Iranian Revolutionary Guards official warned that any renewed U.S. attack on Iran, even a limited one, would trigger 'long and painful strikes' on American positions in the region. The warning, paired with Tehran's continued claim over the Strait of Hormuz, complicated U.S.-led efforts to build a maritime coalition and raised the stakes around stalled ceasefire talks.
dl: THREAT — Iran is publicly threatening sustained strikes on U.S. regional positions if Washington resumes attacks, increasing escalation risk around the blockade standoff

sources.name: Reuters via West Hawaii Today, May 1

title: U.S. and partners advance Maritime Freedom Construct for Hormuz
detail: France's foreign minister said a new U.S.-led coalition, dubbed the Maritime Freedom Construct, would complement a separate U.K.-France initiative to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. The announcement signaled that Western governments were preparing a coordinated maritime push while Gulf states remained wary of any unilateral Iranian arrangement over the waterway.
dl: STRATEGIC — Western coalition-building raises pressure on Iran's Hormuz restrictions and could set conditions for renewed naval confrontation

title: Iran sends latest peace proposal via Pakistan; Trump says he is not satisfied
detail: Iran delivered its latest negotiating proposal to the United States through Pakistani mediators on April 30, Iranian state media said, but President Trump told reporters on May 1 that he was not satisfied with what Tehran was offering. Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Iran remained open to diplomacy if Washington changed its approach, leaving the extended ceasefire intact but talks unresolved.
dl: STRATEGIC — The main diplomatic channel remains alive but insufficient to unlock the Hormuz and nuclear deadlock

title: Israel and Hezbollah trade new strikes despite Lebanon ceasefire
detail: Israel carried out several airstrikes on southern Lebanon, killing at least four people in three villages, while Hezbollah said it launched rockets and drones at Israeli military positions. Israeli media reported a drone strike near Margaliot caused a fire and two Israeli soldiers were lightly wounded by a separate Hezbollah drone impact, underscoring the continued erosion of the April 17 ceasefire.
dl: HEAVY — The Lebanon ceasefire is fraying into active cross-border exchanges, with fresh Lebanese fatalities and Israeli military casualties on the same day

title: Hezbollah says it reinforced fighters and weapons in south Lebanon
detail: Hezbollah media relations director Youssef Al Zein said the group had moved reinforcements and weapons into southern Lebanon during the battle with Israel despite earlier disarmament south of the Litani River. He said the forces did not use roads controlled by the Lebanese army and argued Israeli advances were possible because Hezbollah infrastructure south of the Litani had been destroyed, adding to signs that the Lebanon ceasefire was fraying.
dl: MODERATE — Hezbollah reinforcement claims point to renewed force regeneration on the Lebanon front despite ceasefire and disarmament terms

sources.name: The Hindu liveblog, May 1 (AFP item 20:22 IST)

title: China urges lasting U.S.-Iran ceasefire and reopening of Hormuz
detail: China's U.N. ambassador Fu Cong said maintaining the Iran war ceasefire was an urgent necessity and warned against renewed attacks. He called for Iran to lift restrictions on the Strait of Hormuz and for the United States to lift its naval blockade, saying the issue would likely be high on the agenda if the strait remained closed when President Trump visits China.
dl: STRATEGIC — Beijing publicly presses both sides to preserve the ceasefire and reopen Hormuz as global economic pressure mounts

sources.name: The Hindu liveblog, May 1 (Reuters item 22:44 IST)

title: Mojtaba Khamenei calls for economic battle as blockade pressure deepens
detail: Iranian supreme leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei urged Iranians to wage an economic and cultural battle to "disappoint" enemies, prioritise domestic goods, and avoid layoffs at damaged businesses. The statement came as the U.S. blockade disrupted Iranian oil shipments and inflation reportedly rose past 50%, showing Tehran shifting public messaging toward resilience under siege.
dl: STRATEGIC — Iran frames blockade and sanctions as an economic war, signaling prolonged resistance rather than near-term capitulation

sources.name: The Hindu liveblog, May 1 (AFP item 23:37 IST)

title: Saudi liquor shortage highlights Iran war shipping bottlenecks
detail: Reuters reported that Saudi Arabia's only official alcohol shop in Riyadh had run short of beer, wine and spirits after Iran war disruption delayed shipments from Bahrain and the United Arab Emirates. Long queues and bare shelves at the diplomatic-quarter store showed the Hormuz bottleneck spilling into Gulf supply chains beyond energy cargoes.
dl: STRATEGIC — Hormuz and Gulf shipping disruption is now visibly affecting niche civilian supply chains in Saudi Arabia, reinforcing wider economic pressure from the war

title: UNHCR says Iran war shipping disruption is delaying refugee aid
detail: Reuters reported that UNHCR warned the Iran war had more than doubled the cost of sending some aid to Sudan and was delaying deliveries by weeks. Insecurity around the Strait of Hormuz, port congestion, higher fuel prices and war-risk insurance pushed a 2,018-ton relief shipment cost estimate from $927,000 to $1.87 million, forcing reroutes through Jordan and Oman while the agency remained severely underfunded.
dl: STRATEGIC — Hormuz disruption is now materially slowing humanitarian logistics beyond the Gulf, widening the war's civilian impact despite the ceasefire

sources.name: The Hindu liveblog, May 1 (Reuters item 18:17 IST)

title: UN chief warns Hormuz curbs are strangling the global economy
detail: U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres warned that the consequences of the West Asia war were growing dramatically worse, saying restrictions on navigational rights around the Strait of Hormuz were disrupting energy, transport, manufacturing and food markets and 'strangling' the global economy. He called for dialogue and measures to open a pathway back from the brink.
dl: STRATEGIC — The U.N. frames the Hormuz closure as a broad global economic threat, increasing diplomatic pressure on both Washington and Tehran

sources.name: The Hindu liveblog, May 1 (UN chief item 19:16 IST)

title: U.S. Treasury warns shippers not to pay Iran Hormuz tolls
detail: The U.S. Treasury's OFAC warned that any U.S. or foreign shipper paying Iran for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz could face sanctions, including payments disguised as charitable donations to the Iranian Red Crescent, Bonyad Mostazafan or embassy accounts. Reuters reported the advisory as Iran's latest proposal left toll or fee demands tied to reopening the strait, with at least one reported $2 million payment already made for transit.
dl: STRATEGIC — Washington is trying to deny Tehran revenue and leverage from any partial Hormuz reopening, making shipping companies choose between Iranian passage demands and U.S. sanctions risk

title: U.S. sanctions Qingdao terminal and dark-fleet tanker over Iranian oil
detail: The U.S. State Department sanctioned Qingdao Haiye Oil Terminal Co., its president Xinchun Li, vessel managers and the Panama-flagged tanker New Fusion, accusing the network of handling tens of millions of barrels of sanctioned Iranian crude and petroleum products through ship-to-ship transfers and dark-fleet operations. The move marked another round of Economic Fury pressure aimed at Iran's oil revenues even as ceasefire diplomacy remained stalled.
dl: STRATEGIC — The U.S. is extending Iran-war economic pressure deeper into China's oil logistics network, raising the cost of sanctions evasion during the Hormuz blockade standoff

title: Trump formally tells Congress Iran hostilities have terminated at War Powers deadline
detail: President Trump sent letters to House Speaker Mike Johnson and Senate president pro tempore Chuck Grassley saying there had been no exchange of fire between U.S. forces and Iran since April 7 and that the hostilities that began February 28 had terminated. The notice landed on the 60-day War Powers deadline while the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports continued and more than 50,000 U.S. service members remained deployed in the region, sharpening the legal dispute over whether the ceasefire ends or pauses congressional authorization requirements.
dl: STRATEGIC — The White House converts its War Powers argument into a formal congressional notice while retaining regional force posture and blockade pressure

title: Lebanon says Israeli campaign death toll has reached 2,618
detail: Lebanon's Ministry of Public Health said the death toll from Israel's airstrikes and ground operations in Lebanon since March 2 had reached 2,618, with 8,094 wounded, including at least 184 children and 296 women killed as of Thursday. Israel says it is targeting Hezbollah and has continued evacuation orders and operations in the south, while Hezbollah and Israeli forces accuse each other of violating the April 17 ceasefire.
dl: HEAVY — The Lebanon front's cumulative civilian and military toll underscores that the Israel-Hezbollah war is continuing despite ceasefire claims

title: Fourteen Iranian soldiers killed clearing unexploded ordnance in Zanjan
detail: Local media reported that 14 Iranian soldiers were killed and two wounded during operations to defuse unexploded ordnance in northwestern Zanjan province. Officials said IRGC teams were clearing cluster-munition contamination from recent airstrikes, including around 1,200 hectares of agricultural land, and had already neutralized more than 15,000 explosive items before the deadly incident.
dl: HEAVY — Unexploded ordnance from the air campaign is now causing significant post-strike military casualties and long-term contamination risks in Iran's northwest

sources.name: Times of India liveblog, May 2 00:40 IST

title: CENTCOM says 45 commercial vessels have been turned back under Iran blockade
detail: U.S. Central Command said U.S. forces enforcing the naval blockade of Iranian ports had directed 45 commercial vessels to turn around or return to port. The figure gave the clearest public scale marker yet for the blockade's maritime enforcement as diplomacy over Hormuz and Iran's oil exports remained stalled.
dl: STRATEGIC — The U.S. blockade is visibly constraining commercial traffic at scale, adding maritime and economic pressure while raising risks of shipping incidents

sources.name: Times of India liveblog, May 1 23:03 IST

title: U.S. Navy expands AI mine-hunting program for Hormuz threat
detail: Reuters reported that the U.S. Navy awarded Domino Data Lab a contract worth up to $99.7 million to expand Project AMMO, an accelerated machine-learning program intended to make underwater mine detection faster, more accurate and less dependent on sailors and ships. The award came as Navy planners prioritized options for clearing Iranian mines in the Strait of Hormuz during the blockade crisis.
dl: STRATEGIC — Washington is investing in faster mine-clearance capacity for Hormuz, signaling preparation for a prolonged or potentially contested reopening of the strait

title: CNN investigation says Iran and allies damaged 16 U.S. military sites
detail: CNN reported, based on satellite imagery and interviews with U.S. and Gulf officials, that Iran and allied forces damaged at least 16 U.S. military installations across eight Middle Eastern countries during the war, including radar, communications and aircraft systems. The investigation said some facilities were severely degraded or partially unusable, with internal U.S. cost estimates reportedly reaching $40 billion to $50 billion despite a lower public Pentagon figure.
dl: MAJOR — The assessment indicates Iran’s strike campaign caused broader damage to U.S. regional basing than previously visible, especially to high-value radar and communications infrastructure

satelliteImages.description: CNN investigation based on satellite imagery reports damage to at least 16 U.S. military sites across eight Middle Eastern countries

satelliteImages.source: Washington Post satellite imagery analysis via Jerusalem Post
satelliteImages.description: Washington Post review of commercial satellite imagery found at least 228 damaged or destroyed structures/equipment items at 15 U.S. military sites across the Gulf and Jordan

title: Trump says U.S.-supplied weapons sent through Kurds did not reach Iran protesters
detail: President Trump said the U.S. had sent a small amount of weapons through Kurdish intermediaries during the Iranian protest wave earlier in the year, but that he was “not happy” with the delivery and believed the Kurds kept them. Times Now reported Trump identified Sulaymaniyah as the transfer point, while a Kurdish opposition spokesman separately told Fox News that his group did not receive weapons during the demonstrations.
dl: STRATEGIC — Trump’s comments expose a disputed covert-support channel tied to Iran’s protest front and could strain Kurdish intermediaries as Washington weighs renewed pressure on Tehran

title: Iran sends new peace proposal through Pakistan but Trump says he is not satisfied
detail: Iran delivered a new negotiating proposal to the United States through Pakistani mediators, according to Iranian state media and a Pakistani official, but President Trump told reporters he was “not satisfied” and said Tehran was asking for things he could not accept. Reuters reported Trump praised Pakistan’s mediation and said phone negotiations were continuing, while Iran’s foreign minister said Tehran was ready for diplomacy if Washington changed its “excessive approach” but that Iranian forces remained ready for any renewed attack.
dl: STRATEGIC — A fresh Pakistan-mediated proposal shows diplomacy is still alive, but Trump’s rejection keeps the ceasefire fragile and the Hormuz blockade unresolved

title: U.S. warns shippers not to pay Iranian Hormuz tolls, even through charities
detail: The U.S. Treasury’s OFAC warned U.S. and non-U.S. shippers that paying Iran for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz could trigger sanctions regardless of payment method, including fiat currency, digital assets, offsets, informal swaps or nominal donations to groups such as the Iranian Red Crescent. The advisory followed Iranian proposals for tolls or fees as part of a war-ending arrangement and reports that at least one $2 million safe-passage payment had already been made.
dl: STRATEGIC — Washington is trying to prevent Iran from monetizing control of Hormuz, making any private workaround legally risky and hardening the blockade standoff

title: Israel strikes southern Lebanon as Hezbollah drone wounds two Israeli soldiers
detail: Israel carried out new airstrikes across southern Lebanon despite the extended ceasefire, with Lebanon’s Health Ministry saying at least 10 people were killed, including six in Habboush near Nabatiyeh and four in other southern villages. Hezbollah said it launched rockets and drones at Israeli military positions, and Israel’s military confirmed an explosive drone fell near the border; Israeli media reported two soldiers were lightly wounded in a separate drone impact near Margaliot.
dl: HEAVY — The Lebanon front continues to produce lethal exchanges under a nominal ceasefire, with both Israeli airstrikes and Hezbollah drones expanding the risk of renewed escalation

title: Khamenei calls for economic battle as blockade pressure and inflation mount
detail: Iran’s supreme leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei urged Iranians to wage an “economic and cultural jihad” to disappoint and defeat enemies, calling for priority consumption of domestic goods and asking owners of damaged businesses to avoid layoffs. The statement came as U.S. port blockades disrupted Iranian oil shipments and The Hindu/AFP cited official data showing inflation had risen past 50% in recent weeks.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran is framing the war’s economic shock as a domestic mobilization campaign, signaling sustained pressure from sanctions, damaged businesses and oil-export disruption

sources.name: The Hindu live updates / AFP, May 1

title: U.S. sanctions Iranian exchange houses over shadow-banking war finance
detail: The U.S. Treasury's OFAC designated three Iranian foreign currency exchange houses — Opal Exchange, Radin Exchange and Tahayyori / Arz Iran Exchange — along with associated owners and front companies under the Economic Fury campaign. Treasury said the networks convert oil revenues, often settled in Chinese yuan, into usable foreign currency for Iranian banks, the military and partners or proxies, moving billions of dollars through shadow-banking channels despite sanctions.
dl: STRATEGIC — Washington is expanding the Iran-war pressure campaign from shipping and oil logistics into Tehran's currency-conversion networks, aiming to cut off funds that sustain the military and sanctions evasion

title: USS Gerald R. Ford leaves Middle East as U.S. maintains Iran blockade
detail: The USS Gerald R. Ford carrier strike group departed the Middle East after an unusually long deployment tied to Iran-war operations, with reports saying it had moved into the U.S. European Command area and was expected to return to Norfolk around mid-May. The move reduces one layer of U.S. carrier firepower near Iran, though live updates and regional reporting said roughly 20 U.S. Navy ships, including the Abraham Lincoln and George H.W. Bush carrier groups, remained deployed to enforce the blockade and pressure Tehran over Hormuz.
dl: STRATEGIC — The Ford's departure slightly thins U.S. naval surge capacity while leaving the blockade architecture intact, showing Washington is balancing crew strain and maintenance needs against continued coercive pressure on Iran

title: Report: Pezeshkian and Ghalibaf seek Araghchi ouster over IRGC role in talks
detail: Iran International reported that President Masoud Pezeshkian and parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf were seeking Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi's removal, accusing him of following Revolutionary Guard chief Ahmad Vahidi's instructions in nuclear talks without keeping the president informed. Indian outlets and live coverage amplified the report as Iran's latest Pakistan-mediated proposal was reaching Washington, highlighting friction between elected officials, the foreign ministry and the IRGC over negotiation strategy.
dl: STRATEGIC — A fight over Araghchi would expose serious command-and-control tensions inside Tehran's negotiating camp just as Iran is trying to sell a revised peace proposal and manage pressure from the blockade

title: Fourteen IRGC soldiers killed clearing unexploded ordnance in Zanjan
detail: Iranian media, citing the Revolutionary Guards, said 14 soldiers were killed and two wounded during a mission to defuse unexploded ordnance in northwestern Zanjan province. The Guards said enemy airstrikes using cluster bombs and aerial mines had contaminated roughly 1,200 hectares of farmland, and that clearance teams had already neutralized more than 15,000 explosive items before the deadly detonation.
dl: HEAVY — The deaths show that unexploded cluster munitions from the air campaign remain a lethal hazard inside Iran even during the ceasefire, tying down IRGC specialists and threatening civilians and farmland long after strikes end

sources.name: Times of India liveblog, May 2 00:40 IST

title: Trump tells Congress Iran hostilities have “terminated” despite continuing blockade
detail: President Donald Trump notified House Speaker Mike Johnson and Senate president pro tempore Chuck Grassley that hostilities with Iran had ended, writing that there had been no exchange of fire between U.S. forces and Iran since April 7 and that the February 28 hostilities had “terminated.” The declaration came as the War Powers deadline arrived, while U.S. forces remained deployed, the naval blockade continued and live coverage noted Trump still warned Iran posed a significant threat.
dl: STRATEGIC — The letter is a legal and political maneuver to preserve the Iran campaign and blockade without seeking new congressional authorization, underscoring that the ceasefire has not resolved the underlying military standoff

sources.name: Times of India liveblog, May 2 01:12 IST

title: Khamenei and IRGC vow new Iranian management rules for Strait of Hormuz
detail: Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei declared a “new chapter” for the Strait of Hormuz, saying Tehran would introduce new management measures after U.S.-Israeli actions against Iran. The IRGC Navy followed with a statement saying the equations and rules for the new management of the Persian Gulf had been set and would be enforced along Iran’s coastline and the Strait, even as Washington warned shippers against paying Iran-linked Hormuz tolls.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran is turning control of Hormuz into a formal postwar bargaining lever, signaling that maritime restrictions and toll enforcement may persist unless the U.S. blockade and sanctions pressure are lifted

sources.name: Times of India liveblog, May 2 03:21 IST

title: Iran executes two men accused of spying for Israel near Natanz
detail: Iranian media, citing the judiciary, said Yaghoub Karimpour and Nasser Bakarzadeh were hanged after being convicted of cooperating with Israel and Mossad. Reports said one man was accused of collecting intelligence near the Natanz nuclear facility in Isfahan province while the other was accused of passing sensitive information and gathering details on officials, religious figures and key sites.
dl: STRATEGIC — The executions show Tehran intensifying internal counterintelligence and deterrence around nuclear sites, a sign that the Israeli covert-threat narrative remains central despite the ceasefire

title: Pentagon orders 5,000-troop drawdown from Germany amid Iran-war rift
detail: The Pentagon said Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth ordered the United States to remove about 5,000 troops from Germany within the next year, following President Trump’s threats to scale back the U.S. military presence there. German officials framed the move as a reason for Europe to strengthen its own defenses, while NATO said it was working with Washington to understand the force-posture decision after tensions over the Iran war strained U.S.-German relations.
dl: STRATEGIC — The Iran war is now reshaping U.S. force posture in Europe, widening NATO political fractures and potentially shifting military capacity away from Germany while Washington keeps pressure on Tehran

sources.name: Times of India liveblog, May 2 03:26 IST

title: Israeli strikes kill seven in southern Lebanon despite fragile ceasefire
detail: Lebanon's state-run National News Agency said Israeli airstrikes killed at least seven people and wounded others in southern Lebanon, even as the Lebanon-Israel ceasefire remained formally in place. Reported strikes included a car in Kfar Dajal that killed two people, a home in Lwaizeh that killed three, and a strike on Shoukin that killed two more.
dl: HEAVY — The continued Israeli strike pattern keeps the Lebanon front active during the Iran-war ceasefire period, raising the risk that Hezbollah-linked retaliation or ceasefire-monitoring failures could reopen a wider northern front

title: U.S. warns shippers not to pay Iranian Strait of Hormuz transit fees
detail: The U.S. warned shipping companies that they could face sanctions if they make payments to Iran for safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz. OFAC said prohibited payments could include cash, digital assets, offsets, informal swaps, in-kind transfers, charitable donations, or payments routed through Iranian embassies, targeting Tehran's attempt to monetize safer routes near its coastline after restricting the strait.
dl: STRATEGIC — Washington is trying to criminalize Iran's emerging Hormuz toll system, escalating the economic blockade from Iranian ports and oil exports to the payment channels that keep maritime traffic moving

sources.name: Hindustan Times liveblog, May 2 18:17 / May 3 01:07 IST
CNA background link to U.S. Treasury / Hormuz sanctions, May 2

title: China says it will not comply with U.S. sanctions on refineries buying Iranian oil
detail: China's Commerce Ministry said U.S. sanctions against five Chinese firms targeted for purchasing Iranian oil 'shall not be recognised, implemented, or complied with.' The injunction covered Shandong Jincheng Petrochemical, Shandong Shouguang Luqing Petrochemical, Shandong Shengxing Chemical, Hengli Petrochemical (Dalian) Refinery and Hebei Xinhai Chemical Group, as Washington continued trying to choke off Tehran's oil revenue.
dl: STRATEGIC — Beijing's refusal directly challenges the U.S. economic-pressure campaign and preserves a major outlet for discounted Iranian crude, limiting the blockade's leverage before Trump-Xi talks

title: Spirit Airlines halts operations after Iran-war fuel shock and failed bailout
detail: Bankrupt budget carrier Spirit Airlines ceased operations, becoming the first major U.S. airline casualty linked to the Iran war after failing to secure creditor backing for a proposed $500 million government bailout. Live reporting said the shutdown was driven by the wartime surge in jet-fuel prices and would force customers to rebook through other carriers while threatening thousands of jobs.
dl: STRATEGIC — The shutdown shows the Iran war's energy shock is now producing visible domestic economic casualties in the United States, turning blockade-driven fuel costs into a political liability for Washington

title: Iran says U.S. must choose diplomacy or confrontation after new proposal
detail: Iran's deputy foreign minister for legal and international affairs Kazem Gharibabadi told diplomats in Tehran that the 'ball is in the United States' court' to choose negotiations or continued confrontation. He said Iran remained prepared for both paths while also saying Tehran would maintain pessimism and distrust toward America's honesty in diplomacy.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran is framing the next escalation decision as Washington's responsibility while preserving a threat of renewed confrontation, keeping pressure on the U.S. to respond to the Pakistan-mediated proposal

title: Trump reviews new Iran proposal while warning renewed strikes remain possible
detail: President Donald Trump said he had been briefed on a proposed Iran deal and was waiting for the exact wording, while also warning that renewed strikes remained possible if Tehran 'misbehaves.' The comments followed reporting that Tehran had sent a revised proposal through Pakistan that would reopen Hormuz and end the U.S. blockade before nuclear talks.
dl: STRATEGIC — The ceasefire is entering a decision point: Washington is studying Iran's proposal but explicitly keeping military strikes on the table, so the diplomatic channel has not removed escalation risk

title: Trump says Iran's 14-point peace plan likely unacceptable as Tehran warns renewed war is likely
detail: After Iran reportedly sent a 14-point proposal through Pakistan to end the war across all fronts and create a new framework for the Strait of Hormuz, President Donald Trump wrote that he would review the plan but 'can't imagine that it would be acceptable,' saying Iran had not paid a big enough price. Iranian central-command official Mohammad Jafar Asadi separately told Fars that renewed conflict with the United States was likely because Washington could not be trusted to keep agreements.
dl: STRATEGIC — The Pakistan-mediated channel is still alive, but Trump's public skepticism and Iran's renewed-war warning mark a sharp deterioration from simple review of the proposal toward a possible collapse of the ceasefire process

sources.name: Hindustan Times closed liveblog summary, May 3 06:18 / 08:03 IST

title: Iranian supertanker carrying $220 million in oil slips past U.S. blockade
detail: TankerTrackers.com reported that the National Iranian Tanker Company VLCC HUGE had evaded the U.S. naval blockade and reached the Asia-Pacific region with more than 1.9 million barrels of crude worth nearly $220 million. The ship was last seen off Sri Lanka more than a week earlier and was moving through Indonesia's Lombok Strait toward the Riau Archipelago after going dark on AIS since March 20; Iranian state media separately claimed at least 52 Iranian ships had broken the blockade since April 29, while the U.S. military said it had forced about 41 Iran-linked vessels to turn back.

Lawmaker Ebrahim Azizi said only commercial vessels and parties cooperating with Iran would benefit, while First Vice President Mohammad Reza Aref said Iran would no longer allow enemy military equipment through the strait; state TV said European countries had begun talks with the IRGC navy over transit.
dl: STRATEGIC — Tehran is formalizing selective control of Hormuz into a toll-and-permission system, challenging freedom of navigation while offering cooperative states a path through the chokepoint

Emirati officials said two other drones were stopped after entering from the western border and that the UAE reserved the right to respond; Saudi Arabia separately said it intercepted three drones that entered from Iraqi airspace. UAE adviser Anwar Gargash framed the Barakah incident as a dangerous escalation by the principal perpetrator or one of its agents, while investigations continued.
